content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful for a second opinion or a paper-style sketch.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). Background dispatch leaves the parent's session log stuck on "running in background" on the dashboard.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no mandatory dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and clears the file after the plan phase succeeds. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Plan Agent
2	
3	You are the plan agent. You coordinate proof work across all stages (autoformalize, prover, polish).
4	
5	## Iteration number
6	
7	Your invocation prompt contains a line `Archon iteration: NNN`. That is the canonical counter — written to `logs/iter-NNN/`, stamped into commit messages, and exposed to subagent tools as `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` in the environment.
8	
9	The session counter under `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` is independent.
10	
11	## What the loop has already done for you
12	
13	Everything below is pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do NOT need to "go read" any of these files — acting on them is enough.
14	
15	- **User hints** captured from `USER_HINTS.md` (cleared after your phase succeeds).
16	- **Blueprint-doctor findings** from the prior iter (orphan chapters, broken `\ref`/`\uses`, new axioms).
17	- **Recent iter sidecars** (last few iters' `plan.md` / `review.md`).
18	- **Subagent catalog** (every enabled subagent's name + description + dispatcher rules — the authoritative roster for this iter; do NOT `ls .archon/subagents/`).
19	- **References summary** from `references/summary.md` when present.
20	
21	## Your Job
22	
23	1. Read the injected blocks above.
24	2. Collect prover results from `task_results/<file>.md`; merge findings into `task_pending.md` (attempts) and `task_done.md` (resolved). Clear processed result files. (Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the loop.)
25	3. Read `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` for context — do not repeat documented dead ends.
26	4. Read `proof-journal/sessions/` for the latest session's `summary.md` + `recommendations.md`. Read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if present.
27	5. **Read and revise `STRATEGY.md`** before writing prover objectives or invoking subagents (see "Long-arc Strategy" below).
28	6. For each active task: completed? feasible? if not, why? does a subagent in your catalog help?
29	7. Trust the loop's deterministic sorry-count + commit metadata. Spot-check independently only when a prover's self-report is internally inconsistent.
30	8. Replace unreasonable tasks (impossible / wrong approach) with corrected plans in `PROGRESS.md`.
31	9. **Write informal proof into the blueprint** (see "Blueprint chapters" below). Keep blueprint and Lean consistent.
32	10. Optionally invoke subagents (see "Subagent delegation" below). Mandatory ones in your catalog are tagged `[MANDATORY]` — you MUST dispatch them this phase.
33	11. Set self-contained objectives for the next prover round in `PROGRESS.md`.
34	12. Do NOT write formal proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself. If you find yourself starting to, stop and return to coordination.
35	13. Detect and address project-wide critical issues (wrong definitions, false statements, flawed strategies, axioms) — even when long-present.
36	
37	## Write permissions and boundaries
38	
39	You may write `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`, `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`. You must NOT edit `.lean` files, `task_results/` files, or `USER_HINTS.md` (the loop manages that one for you).
40	
41	**Escalation channels**:
42	- **Iter sidecar** `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` — full escalation context.
43	- **PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives`** — when intentionally skipping provers (escalation pending, hard gate fired), write the marker `(no prover dispatch this iter — see iter/iter-NNN/plan.md for rationale)` in the section. The plan-validate hook recognizes this as intentional.
44	- **TO_USER.md** — owned by review; do NOT write directly. Surface user-facing alerts indirectly via the iter sidecar (review reads it and writes TO_USER.md).
45	
46	**`## Current Objectives` is for files the prover should work on — nothing else.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per `.lean` file referenced there. Off-limits files belong in a separate section.
47	
48	**Blueprint gate** (before listing any file F in `## Current Objectives`): the corresponding blueprint chapter must be complete + correct per the catalog's latest blueprint-review status. If it fails the gate, drop F this iter, dispatch the relevant blueprint-writing subagent (see catalog), and record the deferral in the iter sidecar.
49	
50	**Diligence**: never choose laziness. Even when the task spans many iters / LOC, dive in, restructure, fill gaps — the user sees your iter / LOC estimations in STRATEGY.md and expects effort that matches them.
51	
52	**No new axioms.** If axioms are already present, remove them. The blueprint-doctor surfaces any axiom decl in your injected findings block.
53	
54	## Boundary: mathematical intent, not Lean syntax
55	
56	Your output is mathematical intent. The prover's output is Lean syntax. Never cross this boundary.
57	
58	- **You MAY** use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` to check whether a piece of Mathlib infrastructure *exists*.
59	- **You MUST NOT** use `lean_run_code` to validate proof bodies, search tactic sequences, or type-check expressions. If you find yourself writing or testing Lean tactic code, stop — that is the prover's job.
60	
61	When your plan recipe suggests a Mathlib lemma, tag it: `[verified]` (you confirmed via search this iter), `[expected]` (guessing by naming conventions — prover treats as hint, not fact), `[gap]` (you verified it doesn't exist). Past iters' verification does NOT carry forward; Mathlib bumps rename and remove things.
62	
63	## Protected declarations
64	
65	`archon-protected.yaml` lists the mathematician's read-only surface. No agent may modify protected signatures. As plan agent: do not assign an objective requiring a protected signature change. Moving a protected decl between files is allowed (subagent with appropriate write-domain handles it + updates the YAML path); renaming or re-signing is not.
66	
67	## References
68	
69	`references/summary.md` is injected. Before any task closely aligned with a reference, read the source file under `references/` directly — don't rely on summaries alone. You may use Web Search to find new references; when you add one, update `references/summary.md`.
70	
71	## Blueprint chapters
72	
73	Informal proofs live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file (`Foo/Bar.lean` → `Foo_Bar.tex`). `blueprint/src/content.tex` `\input`s the chapters; keep it updated. Each chapter contains rigorous prose at textbook level — not sketches.
74	
75	Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the content the prover needs. Each declaration block looks like:
76	
77	```latex
78	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
79	  \label{thm:some_label}
80	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
81	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
82	  Informal statement, in standard mathematical notation.
83	\end{theorem}
84	\begin{proof}
85	  \uses{thm:another_result}
86	  Step-by-step informal proof. Detail enough to formalize.
87	\end{proof}
88	```
89	
90	**Proof sketches must be mathematical, not syntactic.** No Lean tactics.
91	
92	**Markers** are managed deterministically — `\leanok` by the `sync_leanok` phase between prover and review, `\mathlibok` by the review agent. **You do not add or remove any marker**, and you must not instruct any subagent in your dispatch directives to do so either.
93	
94	**LaTeX macros**: define in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using.
95	
96	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective record which chapter backs it: `**`Foo.lean`** — Blueprint: `chapters/Foo.tex` (theorems `thm:x`, `thm:y`)`.
97	
98	## Long-arc Strategy
99	
100	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from the current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it. The mathematician reads it — keep it human-readable.
101	
102	Read it early every iteration. Update it after processing prover/review results, before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint.
103	
104	### Canonical structure (use this skeleton)
105	
106	`STRATEGY.md` follows a fixed, bounded structure. Use these headings in this order. Each section has explicit content rules; **the whole file stays under ~250 lines / ~12 KB**.
107	
108	```markdown
109	# Strategy
110	
111	## Goal
112	<two or three sentences naming the final theorem(s). NOT a paragraph of
113	motivation; just the destination. Cite by name, not by handwave.>
114	
115	## Phases & estimations
116	<one Markdown table, one row per remaining phase / route, rough order.
117	Columns: Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks.
118	Concise cells — one short line each. Drop rows for completed phases.
119	Aim for 4–10 rows.>
120	
121	## Routes
122	<only if the strategy admits multiple routes. One short subsection per
123	still-live route. Each: 3–6 lines naming the route, the pivot that
124	selected it, and the milestones marking its completion. NO Lean code,
125	NO blueprint excerpts. If single route, write "single route" here.>
126	
127	## Open strategic questions
128	<one-line bullets. Questions tracked but not yet decisions. Maximum ~8.
129	If you have more, you're using this as a scratchpad — move to iter sidecar.>
130	
131	## Mathlib gaps & new material
132	<one-line bullets, split into "Gaps to fill" (Mathlib pieces to build)
133	and "New project material" (defs/structures/lemmas introduced by the
134	project). Maximum ~12 total. Name the missing concept — NOT its
135	definition.>
136	```
137	
138	### Hard rules
139	
140	- **No Lean code, no blueprint excerpts, no proof sketches.** Those live in chapters.
141	- **No per-iter narrative.** No "this iter we tried X", no revision log. That history lives in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`.
142	- **No accumulation.** When a phase completes, delete its row. When a route is excised, remove its subsection. STRATEGY.md shrinks toward "complete"; it does NOT grow.
143	- **No long prose in table cells.** One short line per cell.
144	- **No "appendix" sections** (Historical decisions, Considered alternatives, Past iterations summary). Iter sidecars hold the alternatives that were rejected.
145	
146	### When to edit
147	
148	Edit STRATEGY.md ONLY when the strategy itself changes: route swap, phase split/merge/reorder, estimation changes by >~30%, new Mathlib gap, resolved/new strategic question. Otherwise leave it alone.
149	
150	## Per-iteration sidecars
151	
152	The injected `## Per-iteration sidecars` block names where you write this iter's narrative (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`) and shows the last few iters' sidecars verbatim. Per-iter narrative goes there — not into STRATEGY.md, not into `task_pending.md`. `task_pending.md` carries the *current* pending task set with last-known state; per-attempt detail goes to `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md`.
153	
154	## Feasibility gate
155	
156	For difficult tasks: think harder. Align with `references/`. Use toy examples, analogies, alternative perspectives. Never delegate difficulty to "next iter" or "the prover".
157	
158	Question your previous work. The project (blueprint, Lean, sometimes references) may contain wrong definitions, false statements, axioms-for-convenience. If you identify a critical issue — new or long-present — address it. The catalog has subagents for restructuring; pick the appropriate one.
159	
160	For obstacles, decide whether Mathlib has the infrastructure or whether you need to fill a gap. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for existence checks only — not proof exploration. The informal agent and Web Search are valid for alternative routes. If filling a Mathlib gap is the only viable path, don't avoid it.
161	
162	## Stuck routes and deeper-think triggers
163	
164	Your catalog includes a [MANDATORY] convergence critic whose verdict is per active route. Build its directive from your own extracted signals (sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses, recurring blocker phrases for the last K iters); read its descriptor for the directive format. Verdicts and the required response:
165	
166	- **CONVERGING / UNCLEAR** — proceed.
167	- **CHURNING** — STOP. Do not add more helpers. Execute the critic's named corrective this iter.
168	- **STUCK** — STOP. Route pivot is on the table; execute the corrective.
169	
170	If you believe the verdict is wrong, you may rebut it — but the rebuttal must be EXPLICIT in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, citing the signals you disagree with and your alternative read. Silent overrides are forbidden. Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the failure pattern the critic exists to prevent.
171	
172	Common correctives the critic names: expand the blueprint chapter, consult Mathlib idioms, refactor a load-bearing definition, pivot routes, escalate to the user. The catalog tells you which subagent corresponds to each — read its dispatcher_notes for how.
173	
174	**User escalation requires a fallback.** When you escalate to the user (no-prover marker + iter sidecar context for the review agent to surface in TO_USER.md), you MUST add a `## Fallback if no user response` section to `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming the option you'd pick if forced and what you'll do next iter to execute it. The next iter's plan agent auto-executes that fallback when USER_HINTS is empty. The loop must never stall indefinitely.
175	
176	**Deeper-think trigger summary.** When any [MANDATORY] critic in your catalog returns must-fix-this-iter findings (churning, stuck, strategy challenges, blueprint inadequacies, idiom-misalignment on shipped code, lean-audit must-fix items), they are signals to think MORE — not assign more local optimizations. Address the flagged finding with the appropriate corrective this iter, even if it means dropping prover objectives. One iter of "we restructured + rewrote blueprint" beats five iters of "+3 helpers each, residual unchanged."
177	
178	## Subagent delegation
179	
180	Each subagent in your catalog is one tool. The catalog includes its description, write-domain hint, MANDATORY / read-only / can-spawn flags, and (under "Workflow guidance") its `dispatcher_notes` — *that's the canonical guidance for how to use that subagent*. Read the descriptor's full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing the directive.
181	
182	### How to invoke
183	
184	Pick a kebab-case **slug** (each call within an iter must use a distinct slug — e.g. `split-wlocal`, `m1b-route`). Write the directive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md`, then run via the Bash tool (foreground, one call):
185	
186	```
187	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
188	  --name <subagent-name> \
189	  --slug <slug> \
190	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
191	  --write-domain '<glob>' \
192	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
193	```
194	
195	The wrapper prints a one-line status and exits 0 on success. `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` is set by the loop — no need to pass `--iter-num`.
196	
197	**Dispatch synchronously** (foreground, not `run_in_background`). Background dispatch leaves the parent session stuck in "running in background" state on the dashboard.
198	
199	**Directives must be fully self-contained.** Subagents do not read `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state; they read what you tell them to. Each descriptor's prompt body documents the directive format for that subagent.
200	
201	**Write-domain** globs constrain what the subagent (and any descendants it spawns) may modify. Common: `'Algebra/**'`, `'Algebra/WLocal.lean'`, `'task_results/**'` for read-only subagents. Children's declared domains must be a subset of yours.
202	
203	**Parallelism**: dispatch multiple subagents concurrently by issuing multiple Bash calls in one message. The dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel`.
204	
205	### After each subagent returns
206	
207	The subagent's report lands at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (or `task_results/<parent-slug>/<name>-<slug>.md` when nested). The loop auto-archives it to `logs/iter-NNN/` for the dashboard. You:
208	
209	1. **Read** the full report (the wrapper's stdout summary is compressed).
210	2. **Spot-check** load-bearing claims (the routine sorry-count / compile checks are already done by the loop).
211	3. **Update STRATEGY.md** if findings change the long-arc plan.
212	4. **Update PROGRESS.md** with whatever new objectives the report enables.
213	
214	### Canonical ordering
215	
216	Within a plan phase: read-only critics / precedent consults first, write-capable subagents next, verification / envelope subagents last. **Write prover objectives only after** the subagents have stabilized the definitional landscape.
217	
218	You may invoke a subagent multiple times per iter (distinct slugs each call) when justified.
219	
220	## Informal content for the prover
221	
222	The prover does much better with rich informal guidance. Before assigning a task, ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof.
223	
224	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): in `PROGRESS.md` under the objective.
225	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): in the corresponding `.lean` file as a `/- ... -/` block above the declaration.
226	- **Long content** (full sketch, paper summary, multi-step construction): in the blueprint chapter `.tex`.
227	- **When a reference is vague**: use `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` to generate a sketch, or Web Search to find the paper. Do this *before* assigning the task — never send the prover in blind.
228	
229	Always record in `PROGRESS.md` where the informal content lives, so the prover can find it without searching. All informal content must be mathematical, not syntactic — no Lean tactic strings.
230	
231	## Prover failure modes
232	
233	- **"Mathlib doesn't have it"** — the #1 failure. Do not pass it back with "try harder". Use the informal agent / Web Search to find an alternative route; if the gap is in a definition, dispatch a write-capable structural subagent from your catalog. Update the chapter `.tex` with the re-routed proof before reassigning.
234	- **Wrong construction** — instruct revert (single file) or dispatch a structural subagent (cross-file). Update the chapter first.
235	- **Not using Web Search** — explicitly instruct: "use Web Search to find [arXiv ID], decompose into sub-lemmas, formalize step by step". Update the chapter with the retrieved sketch.
236	- **Early stop on a hard problem** — reject the report. Break into sub-goals in the chapter, assign L1, then L2 after L1 lands.
237	- **Tricks to bypass** (new axioms, ad-hoc weakenings) — reject. Document why this route was chosen and ensure it won't reproduce.
238	- **Repeated blockers** — same blocker over consecutive iters means rewrite the chapter or dispatch a structural subagent. Do NOT re-dispatch the same lane with cosmetic recipe variation.
239	
240	## Verification
241	
242	The loop already runs deterministic checks each iter:
243	
244	- **Sorry count** — stamped into `meta.json` (before/mid/post prover). Do not re-count by hand.
245	- **Axiom check** — runs as part of the blueprint-doctor; new axioms surface in your injected findings block.
246	- **Blueprint consistency** — `sync_leanok` resolves `\lean{...}` against the project decls; the doctor catches broken `\ref` / `\uses`.
247	
248	What's left for you: spot-check inconsistent prover self-reports; act on every entry in the injected doctor findings (or document the deferral); reject any reported completion that left a real `sorry` or introduced a new axiom.
249	
250	## Decomposition strategy
251	
252	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem: read the chapter for sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, …); read related `references/` to align with the original proof; expand the chapter if too thin (informal agent / Web Search); assign one sub-lemma at a time; verify, then assign the next; record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
253	
254	## Multi-agent coordination
255	
256	Provers run in parallel — one per file. Number objectives clearly; each maps to exactly one `.lean` file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside, and **list every ready sorry in that file that the prover should fill in this iter** — not just one:
257	
258	```markdown
259	## Current Objectives
260	
261	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorries in `filter_convergence` (line 156), `filter_inv` (line 188), `filter_assoc` (line 211). Blueprint: `chapters/Core.tex` (`thm:filter_convergence`, `thm:filter_inv`, `thm:filter_assoc`).
262	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `chapters/Measure.tex`.
263	3. **`ChartAlgebra.lean`** — Scaffold the file with declarations for `thm:chart_id`, `thm:chart_comp`, `thm:chart_inv` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`. Blueprint: `chapters/ChartAlgebra.tex`. (File-skeleton dispatch — see below.)
264	```
265	
266	**Agent count = file count.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per file. When a file has multiple ready sorries, list ALL of them under that file's objective — the prover handles them sequentially within one lane. Splitting a multi-sorry file across iters is artificial throttling; one lane working sequentially on three sorries finishes faster than waiting two iters for three single-sorry lanes (the prover keeps its file context warm across sorries).
267	
268	**File-skeleton dispatches.** When a blueprint chapter is complete but the corresponding `.lean` file does not yet exist (or exists but is missing declarations the chapter introduced), it is a legitimate iter objective to dispatch a prover with directive *"scaffold `Foo.lean` with declarations for `thm:a`, `thm:b`, `thm:c` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`; add the import + namespace boilerplate; do not attempt to prove anything yet"*. The next iter then fills the sorries. This is materially faster than one-iter-per-declaration scaffolding.
269	
270	**Mechanical-vs-deep partition.** Sorries split into two regimes:
271	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
272	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
273	
274	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
275	
276	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
277	
278	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
279	
280	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
281	
282	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
283	
284	## Dependency graph
285	
286	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
287	
288	```
289	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
290	```
291	
292	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.
293	
294	## Stage transitions
295	
296	Advance `PROGRESS.md` when all current-stage objectives are met:
297	
298	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (all statements formalized)
299	- `prover` → `polish` (all sorries filled and verified)
300	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (proofs clean, compile)
301

1	# Strategy
2	
3	## Project goal
4	
5	Formalize the nine protected declarations of Christian Merten's Jacobian
6	challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
7	
8	| File | Declaration |
9	|---|---|
10	| `Genus.lean` | `AlgebraicGeometry.genus` |
11	| `Jacobian.lean` | `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian`, `Jacobian.instGrpObj`, `Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus`, `Jacobian.instIsProper`, `Jacobian.instGeometricallyIrreducible` |
12	| `AbelJacobi.lean` | `Jacobian.ofCurve`, `Jacobian.comp_ofCurve`, `Jacobian.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` |
13	
14	All nine signatures are frozen by the mathematician; agents are read-only
15	on them. Importantly, `nonempty_jacobianWitness` quantifies over an
16	arbitrary curve `C : Over (Spec (.of k))` with `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]`
17	— no genus parameter, no $k$-rational-point hypothesis. Any sub-strategy
18	that depends on `C(k) ≠ ∅` (notably `C ≅ ℙ¹_k`) is mathematically false
19	on the protected signature (Brauer–Severi conics over `ℚ` are
20	counterexamples) and must be handled by base change.
21	
22	## End-state (iter-121 pivot)
23	
24	Prior iterations operated under a "ship with one inline `sorry`" end-
25	state, treating each remaining Mathlib gap as project-external and
26	documented-but-deferred. **Per the iter-121 user directive, this framing
27	is dropped**: the project's autonomous loop now operates as a Mathlib
28	contributor, building each missing piece directly in-tree at
29	Mathlib-merge quality and removing the corresponding `sorry`.
30	
31	The end-state is **zero inline `sorry` in the project**, with one
32	**explicit qualification**: **PROVISIONAL on piece (iii)
33	(scheme-level absolute Frobenius) closing in-tree at a tractable LOC
34	cost**. The piece-(iii) named-gap-sorry alternative (per
35	`mathlib-analogist-p1-hedge-iter138` and the iter-139 in-line
36	clarification) is the project's **honest fallback** if and only if
37	the iter-144+ scheme-Frobenius PHANTOM build (~800–1500 LOC; possibly
38	1200–3000 LOC per `strategy-critic-iter140` under-counting CHALLENGE)
39	exceeds project sustainability or returns ≥2 PARTIAL iters. **Under
40	fallback**: the end-state revises to *zero inline `sorry` modulo one
41	named-gap on scheme-level absolute Frobenius* (the named gap sits on
42	a Mathlib-canonical piece; the downstream protected chain carries one
43	residual named-gap identifier). The named-gap alternative
44	is **NOT a stall fallback** (per iter-139) **NOR equivalent to the
45	named-axiom path** (rejected iter-122 by standing rule + iter-126 user
46	hint); it is a **provisional end-state qualification** to be re-decided
47	at the iter-144+ scoping-analogist gate. There are no other deferred
48	tasks; every other gap is on the active roadmap. The roadmap is
49	**multi-year wall-clock honest** (~9–24 months at sustainable
50	50–100 LOC/iter per `strategy-critic-iter140` arithmetic on the
51	17+ iter M2 critical-path + 65–180 iter M3 critical-path), decomposed
52	into milestones M1, M2, M3 with sub-step detail and per-step effort
53	estimates. Iter-by-iter `PROGRESS.md` schedules the next concrete
54	sub-step. **Iter-140 framing-honesty correction (per `strategy-critic-iter140`
55	material concern #2)**: the earlier "multi-month" language under-stated
56	wall-clock by 5–10×; **multi-year** is the honest framing.
57	
58	## Decomposition: genus-stratified body of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`
59	
60	The protected `nonempty_jacobianWitness` has signature
61	
62	```
63	theorem nonempty_jacobianWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
64	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
65	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] :
66	    Nonempty (JacobianWitness C)
67	```
68	
69	Although signatures are frozen, bodies are not. The plan is to
70	restructure the proof body as a genus case-split:
71	
72	```
73	theorem nonempty_jacobianWitness ... := by
74	  by_cases h : AlgebraicGeometry.genus (k := k) C.left = 0
75	  · exact ⟨genusZeroWitness C h⟩      -- closed by milestone M2
76	  · exact ⟨positiveGenusWitness C (Nat.pos_of_ne_zero h)⟩  -- closed by M3
77	```
78	
79	`genus C : ℕ` is decidable-equality, so the `by_cases` is well-formed.
80	This converts M2 from "preparatory infrastructure" into an explicit
81	half-of-a-sorry-closure: the genus-0 sub-theorem `genusZeroWitness`
82	becomes a concrete Lean target. M3 is then framed as "close
83	`positiveGenusWitness`," which is morally what it always was.

413	- **No new axioms.** Every closed declaration must `lean_verify` to

429	- **Sorry-must-be-named-declaration (added iter-143 per `strategy-critic-iter143` Alternative #2 promotion + `lean-auditor-review142` MAJOR + `progress-critic-iter143` CHURNING primary corrective).** A `sorry` must be the body of a **top-level named declaration** (a `theorem`, `def`, `lemma`, or `instance`). It must NOT be embedded inside a `letI`-body, `have`-body, anonymous-`fun`-argument, or any other tactic-/term-bound construction inside a *different* declaration's body. This is because `sorry_analyzer` + `lean_verify` + audit reads all index sorry occurrences at the declaration boundary; a sorry buried inside a `letI` / `have` / anonymous-`fun` body propagates a sorry-tainted value to downstream consumers without surfacing as a `declaration uses sorry` warning of its own, breaking the auditability invariant the project relies on. **Concrete failure mode prevented**: the iter-140 `letI : IsIso ... := isIso_of_app_iso_module _ (fun _ => sorry)` pattern (in `Cotangent/GrpObj.lean:719–721` pre-iter-143) silently propagated a sorry-tainted iso through `simp` consumers, while audit inspection of the wrapping `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_basechange_along_proj_two` declaration missed the taint. **Iter-143 fix (per `refactor-isiso-extract-iter143`)**: extract the residual into a named sorry-bodied theorem `basechange_along_proj_two_inv_app_isIso`; the consuming declaration's `letI` then references the named theorem. **General rule**: any future construction needing a sorry-tainted intermediate must extract the intermediate into a named sorry-bodied lemma BEFORE consuming it; the consuming `letI` / `have` / etc. then references the named declaration, never carries the `sorry` inline. This rule applies project-wide across all milestones (M1/M2/M3/M4); violations are must-fix-this-iter on the lean-auditor's next pass.
430	- **User-hint citation discipline (per `strategy-critic-iter128` sunk-cost flag; CLARIFIED iter-144).** The iter-126 user hint "do the work, no axioms; ~6500–9000 LOC may not be that much for an AI" was issued in the M3-disposition context (M3 Route A vs axiomatising `nonempty_jacobianWitness`). Per the **iter-144 user-hint clarification** the hint means: **write the missing Mathlib material directly in-tree when the loop encounters a gap**; it does NOT mean "post upstream PRs as a project deliverable" and does NOT mean "wait for upstream PRs before continuing". Upstream PR extraction is an OPTIONAL downstream possibility. When this strategy invokes the user hint, it must cite the *specific* decision the hint applies to. Each in-tree expensive build (e.g. scheme-level absolute Frobenius for piece (iii)) must justify itself **on the merits of the local LOC/iter trade-off** vs alternatives, not by appeal to the user hint as a blanket. The hint remains binding *on its actual scope* (M3 disposition + the no-axiom rule + in-tree-not-PR-lane); reasoning that smuggles new scope under it is sunk-cost.
431	- **LOC trigger arm renormalisation discipline (added iter-138 per `strategy-critic-iter138` sunk-cost flag #2; SYMMETRY AMENDMENT iter-141 per `strategy-critic-iter141` must-fix #3).** LOC-based trigger arms (the (a')/(c) revert cap, the fibre-free pivot threshold, etc.) **may renormalise ONLY when a NEW analogist consult justifies a new LOC envelope** (as iter-137's `kaehler-tensorequiv-presheafpullback-iter137` consult did, widening Step 2 from 150–300 LOC to 360–710 LOC). Renormalising on the basis of a prover lane reporting growing LOC without a new analogist re-scope is moving-goalpost and forbidden: "we always raise the cap to fit the envelope" is no trigger at all. Once a new envelope is justified by an analogist consult, **all dependent trigger arms (revert cap, pivot threshold, slip watchpoint) renormalise together with documented arithmetic**; partial renormalisation that tightens one trigger while leaving an arithmetically-adjacent one fixed is sunk-cost-shaped and equally forbidden. **Symmetric direction (iter-141)**: renormalisation discipline applies to BOTH up-and-down adjustments. **When a piece closes materially under its envelope (≥30% under the envelope midpoint), the dependent caps tighten proportionally to "realised LOC + 30% slack"**, regardless of whether the close was within budget. The previous text only ratcheted the cap up; absent symmetric tightening, the LOC arm is a one-way ratchet that partially undoes the iter-138 sunk-cost-flag-#2 prevention over time. Both directions of renormalisation must be documented with arithmetic showing the new cap = realised-LOC × (1 + slack). The slack rate (30%) is fixed; widening it would itself be a renormalisation-discipline violation.
432	- **Consecutive-PARTIAL breakeven counter discipline (added iter-143 per `strategy-critic-iter143` Must-fix #1 + Alternative #3 promotion).** A "breakeven counter" tracks consecutive prover iters that return PARTIAL on the same sub-piece without a strict-count substantive closure. Per `progress-critic-iter141` secondary corrective + `progress-critic-iter142` STUCK-adjacency reinforcement, **5 consecutive PARTIAL iters on the same sub-piece** triggers a route-pivot question (the iter-145+ piece (i.b) Step 2 breakeven pre-commitment). **Counter reset rule (iter-143 NEW)**: a strict-count substantive closure on any sub-sorry of a PARTIAL-routed iter **resets the consecutive-PARTIAL counter by 1** (NOT to 0). Concretely: if the counter reads N entering iter k, and iter k closes ≥1 sub-sorry strict-count substantively (regardless of whether the overall iter outcome is PASS or PARTIAL), the counter for iter k+1 reads max(N−1, 0). **Examples**: iter-142 closed d_map substantively on a PARTIAL iter; counter entering iter-143 reads (iter-138 PARTIAL = 1) + (iter-139 plan-only, no increment) + (iter-140 PARTIAL = 2) + (iter-141 plan-only, no increment) + (iter-142 PARTIAL but strict-count d_map close = 2 + 1 − 1 = **2**); breakeven trigger fires when counter reaches 5, projecting to iter-146+ at earliest under no further closures (one PARTIAL per iter increments the counter by 1; the trigger fires at the iter where counter ≥ 5). **Counter increment rule**: only **active prover-attempt iters** (PARTIAL / FAIL outcomes) increment; plan-only deferral iters (HARD GATE or CHURNING-trigger deferral) do NOT increment (no prover attempt was made). **Rationale**: without the reset rule, the breakeven counter is a one-way ratchet — strict-count closures (genuine technique advances) get no signal credit and the counter monotonically approaches breakeven even on routes that are converging on the residual. The reset-by-1 rule (rather than reset-to-0) ensures (a) genuine closures get signal credit, AND (b) repeated PARTIAL iters still produce a breakeven trigger if the closure rate is below 1-per-iter; net direction-of-travel of the counter measures whether the route is converging on average. **Renormalisation discipline**: per the iter-138 + iter-141 LOC trigger arm rules, this counter rule itself may only renormalise (e.g. change from "reset by 1" to "reset by 2") when justified by a new analogist consult or strategy-critic verdict that names the renormalisation explicitly. The chain has three layers; `df = 0` is established **via cohomological content equivalent to `H^0(C, Ω_{C/k}^{⊕n}) = 0`** (which is equivalent to `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0` over a genus-0 curve), but **NOT via the named theorem "Serre duality"** — Serre duality stays deferred as a piece-(iv) named gap. Concretely:
442	  (1) **Chart-local Kähler-derivation kernel-extraction**: under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot, the proof works chart-by-chart on `C`. For each affine chart `V ⊂ C`, the morphism `f|_V` factors through some affine chart `W ⊂ A`; on the ring level `Γ(W, O_A) → Γ(V, O_C)`, the Kähler-derivation difference `d (f|_V^♯ ϕ) - f|_V^♯ d ϕ` lands in `Γ(V, Ω_{V/k})`. Combining with `KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero` (the chart-algebra (β-core ring-side) sub-piece) and `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n k`-tractable charts (consumer of piece (i.a)), `f|_V^♯ ϕ ∈ range (algebraMap k Γ(V, O_C))` chart-locally.
443	  (2) **Chart-Čech Mayer–Vietoris glueing** (= `H^0(C, Ω_{C/k}^{⊕n}) = 0`-equivalent content): passing from chart-local "lands in `range (algebraMap k ·)`" to a global statement requires a 2-chart Mayer–Vietoris computation on `Ω_{C/k}^{⊕n}`. The project's `Cohomology_MayerVietoris.tex` chapter supplies the Mayer–Vietoris infrastructure used for `Genus.lean`'s `H^1(C, O_C) = 0` computation; the chart-algebra (β-core) sub-piece (`AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart` per the iter-145 blueprint-writer) carries the cotangent variant `H^0(C, Ω_{C/k}^{⊕n}) = 0`. This IS cohomological content equivalent to "global-section-vanishing of `Ω_{C/k}`" on a genus-0 curve; it is **NOT a named-Serre-duality invocation**.
444	  (3) **Char-p Frobenius-iteration patch**: in char p the chart-local Kähler-derivation argument is insufficient when `f|_V` is a p-th-power morphism. Chart-algebra piece (iii) is substituted by `RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm` on each chart's affine ring (the ring-level Frobenius commutation); iterating finitely many times reduces to a separable factor on which the chart-local kernel-extraction concludes. This composes with Layer 1 chart-locally (the iterated Frobenius image lands in the same chart) and does NOT require a new global step.

The honest framing is: **the named-theorem Serre duality is NOT invoked**; piece (iv) Serre duality stays DEFERRED as a named theorem the project does NOT build. **The equivalent cohomological content `H^0(C, Ω_{C/k}^{⊕n}) = 0` IS invoked**, established via a chart-Čech Mayer–Vietoris computation that reuses the project's existing Mayer–Vietoris infrastructure on schemes (analogous to the `H^1(C, O_C) = 0` computation already implemented for `Genus.lean`). The "no Serre duality" claim distinguishes **name-of-theorem** (a multi-thousand-LOC named upstream piece) from **cohomological content** (a single chart-Čech computation reusing existing project Mayer–Vietoris infrastructure).

Detailed analogist context lives in `analogies/chart-algebra-vs-bundled-iter144.md` § "Chart-algebra (β) per-chart translation-invariance argument" and in `analogies/direct-chart-algebra-rigidity-ib-ic.md`.

93	## Directive Format
94	
95	```markdown
96	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
97	
98	## Slug
99	<slug>
100	
101	## Strategy snapshot
102	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy.>
103	
104	## Routes
105	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
106	
107	## References
108	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
109	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
110	
111	## Focus areas (optional)
112	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
113	
114	## Known issues
115	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
116	```
117	
118	## What you do
119	
120	1. **Read your directive completely.**
121	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
122	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
123	   - Read the entire chapter.
124	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
125	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
126	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
127	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
128	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
129	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
130	7. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
131	
132	You may also use:
133	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
134	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
135	
136	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
137	
138	## Report format
139	
140	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
141	
142	```markdown
143	# Blueprint Review Report
144	
145	## Slug
146	<slug>
147	
148	## Iteration
149	<NNN>
150	
151	## Top-level summaries
152	
153	### Incomplete parts
154	<bullets naming chapter + which definition/theorem/proof is missing or shallow. Sorted by severity.>
155	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
156	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
157	
158	### Proofs lacking detail
159	<bullets naming chapter + theorem label + what's vague. Distinct from "incomplete" — the proof exists but isn't enough.>
160	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: jumps from "X holds" to "therefore Y" without naming the lemma used.
161	
162	### Lean difficulty quality
163	<bullets naming `\lean{<name>}` hints whose target is poorly formulated for a prover (too vague, ambiguous, or pointing at something that would lead to a bad type).>
164	- `Foo.tex` / `\lean{Foo.frobnicate}`: signature unclear from prose; prover has no way to infer return type.
165	
166	### Multi-route coverage
167	<one block per route mentioned in directive's Routes. PASS / PARTIAL / MISSING + which chapters cover it.>
168	- Route "via cohomology": PARTIAL — covered in `Cohomology.tex` and `RR.tex` but `Bridge.tex` (which links them) is empty.
169	- Route "via direct computation": MISSING — strategy mentions this as an alternative but no chapter discusses it.
170	
171	## Per-chapter
172	
173	### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex
174	- **complete**: true | partial | false
175	- **correct**: true | partial | false
176	- **notes**:
177	  - <missing | wrong | observation> — <one line>
178	  - ...
179	
180	### blueprint/src/chapters/Bar.tex
181	- **complete**: ...
182	- **correct**: ...
183	- **notes**:
184	  - ...
185	
186	(One block per chapter. Cover every chapter, including the ones that
187	look fine — they get `complete: true`, `correct: true`, `notes: -`.)
188	
189	## Cross-chapter notes
190	
191	- `<chapter A>` defines `\foo` but `<chapter B>`'s proof of `\bar` uses a stronger version.
192	- `<chapter A>` `\lean{Foo.bar}` references a declaration that no longer exists in `Foo.lean` (renamed?).
193	- ...
194	
195	(Use this section for findings that span multiple chapters. Omit if empty.)
196	
197	## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)
198	
199	If you found a definition that, on close reading, conflicts with the strategy in a way that requires a strategy change (not just a chapter rewrite), name it here. These take precedence over everything else; the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md before any Lean work this iter.
200	
201	- `<chapter A>` / `\def:foo`: as currently defined, this <conflict>. STRATEGY.md says <X>, but `foo` actually does <Y>. Resolving requires either changing the strategy or redefining `foo`.
202	
203	## Severity summary
204	
205	Apply these rules verbatim — they decide whether the plan agent dispatches a blueprint-writing subagent (per the catalog) this iter, or defers.
206	
207	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
208	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.
209	  - A route under "Multi-route coverage" is reported as MISSING.
210	  - **Any chapter has `complete: partial | false` OR `correct: partial | false`** — even if the strategy "does not require" that chapter this iter. A `partial` chapter cannot be relied on by any prover; the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent must be dispatched.
211	  - **Any chapter whose `\lean{...}` hint** is marked "Lean difficulty quality: poor" AND the named target is part of an active prover route in PROGRESS.md.
212	  - **Broken `\uses{}` cross-references** that point at non-existent labels — these silently corrupt the dependency graph and must be fixed before provers downstream of them run.
213	- **soon** — cross-cutting items that don't block any specific chapter's prover work yet:
214	  - Lean-difficulty-quality findings for hints NOT in an active prover route.
215	  - Informational cross-chapter style issues, missing `\texttt{...}` decoration mentions, etc.
216	- **informational** — minor observations: naming drift, optional `\lean{...}` references to helpers worth promoting, low-impact prose suggestions.
217	
218	Overall verdict: one sentence.
219	```
220	
221	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly: any **must-fix-this-iter** finding tied to a chapter prevents the corresponding prover from running this iter (see dispatcher_notes for the gate rule). Do not under-classify to avoid blocking provers — the project pays more when a prover formalizes against a wrong/incomplete blueprint than when the prover waits one iter for a writer to land the fix.
222

85	## Directive Format
86	
87	```markdown
88	# Progress Critic Directive
89	
90	## Slug
91	<slug>
92	
93	## Iter
94	<NNN>
95	
96	## Active routes / files under review
97	
98	For each route the planner is considering for this iter's prover work, ONE block:
99	
100	### Route: <name or file path>
101	
102	- **Started at iter**: <NNN>
103	- **Iters audited**: <NNN-K to NNN-1>
104	
105	#### Sorry counts per iter
106	- iter-NNN-K: <count> (e.g. 5)
107	- iter-NNN-(K-1): <count> (e.g. 5)
108	- iter-NNN-(K-2): <count> (e.g. 4)
109	- ...
110	- iter-NNN-1: <count>
111	
112	#### Helpers added per iter
113	- iter-NNN-K: <list or count of new declarations introduced>
114	- ...
115	- iter-NNN-1: <list or count>
116	
117	#### Prover statuses per iter
118	- iter-NNN-K: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary from prover report>
119	- ...
120	- iter-NNN-1: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary>
121	
122	#### Recurring blocker phrases
123	- "<verbatim blocker phrase>" appears in iter-X, iter-Y, iter-Z reports — <one line>
124	- "<verbatim blocker phrase>" appears in iter-X, iter-Y — <one line>
125	- ...
126	
127	#### Strategy estimate vs reality
128	- **`Iters left` from STRATEGY.md** (verbatim from the relevant `## Phases & estimations` row): <e.g. "3">
129	- **Elapsed iters in current phase**: <e.g. "9">
130	- **Phase started at iter**: <e.g. "iter-117 — the iter where this phase row first appeared with its current estimate>
131	
132	#### Planner's current proposal for this iter
133	- <one paragraph: what the planner wants to assign>
134	
135	## PROGRESS.md proposal (this iter)
136	
137	The planner's `## Current Objectives` list it is about to commit. Used for the dispatch-sanity check.
138	
139	- **File count**: <N>
140	- **Files**: <comma-separated basenames>
141	- **Dispatch cap (from --max-objectives)**: <e.g. "10">
142	
143	## Out of scope
144	<routes the planner is NOT considering this iter and does not want assessed>
145	```
146	
147	## What you check
148	
149	For each route's block:
150	
151	1. **Sorry trajectory.** Is the count actually dropping over the K-iter window? "Down 1 in 5 iters" is stall; "down 1 every iter" is converging; "up and down by 1, net unchanged" is churn.
152	
153	2. **Helper accumulation vs payoff.** If helpers are being added but the residual doesn't shrink, that's churn. "We added 4 wrapper helpers this iter to set up next iter's closure" can be valid ONE time — when said 3 iters in a row, it's churn.
154	
155	3. **Recurring blockers.** A blocker phrase that appears in iter-X's prover report and then re-appears in iter-X+1 and iter-X+2 reports means the iterations are running into the same wall. That's STUCK.
156	
157	4. **Prover status pattern.** COMPLETE → COMPLETE → COMPLETE is converging. PARTIAL → PARTIAL → PARTIAL is churn. INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is stuck. PARTIAL → INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is regressing.
158	
159	5. **Planner's proposal.** Is the proposal "another helper round, similar to the last K iters"? If so, and your signals say churn, your verdict is CHURNING and the planner must escalate (blueprint, mathlib analogy, refactor, or pivot). If the proposal differs (refactor, blueprint expansion, route pivot), that's the planner already escalating — credit it.
160	
161	6. **PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity.** Independent of the route-level verdict, sanity-check the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal:
162	
163	   - **Over the dispatch cap**: if file count > the cap shown in the directive (default 10), this is an automatic CHURNING-equivalent finding regardless of route convergence. Runaway fan-out (e.g. 27 provers in one iter) is the failure mode this check exists to prevent. The deterministic `plan_validate` hook caps dispatch downstream of you, but you flag the *planner intent* loudly so the planner self-corrects rather than relying on the safety net every iter.
164	   - **Bloat without route progress**: file count growing iter over iter (e.g. 4 → 7 → 12) while the route signals say CHURNING or STUCK suggests the planner is throwing more provers at the wall instead of escalating. Flag this even if file count is below the cap.
165	
166	   These are dispatch-level checks, not route-level. They land in a separate "PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity" block in your report (see Report format).
167	
168	7. **Throughput honesty.** Compare `Iters left` (verbatim from STRATEGY.md's `## Phases & estimations`) against elapsed iters in the current phase. Bucket:
169	
170	   - **On schedule**: elapsed ≤ estimate.
171	   - **Slipping**: elapsed > estimate but ≤ 2× estimate.
172	   - **Over budget**: elapsed > 2× estimate.
173	   - **Estimate-free**: STRATEGY.md gives no number, or "?", for this row.
174	
175	   "Over budget" with a still-positive `Iters left` is the dishonest-estimate signature — the strategy claims K iters remain but K-many iters have already passed without closure. That's a strategic problem, not a tactical one. Surface it; the planner's corrective is either to revise STRATEGY.md's estimate to honesty or to escalate (pivot, narrow scope). "Estimate-free" with elapsed > 5 iters in a phase is also flag-worthy — every phase row should carry an estimate.
176	
177	   Note: throughput honesty is route-level — it lands in the per-route block, not the dispatch-sanity block.
178	
179	## Verdict rules
180	
181	Apply these rules verbatim:
182	
183	- **CONVERGING**: sorry count strictly decreasing in K-iter window AND no recurring blocker AND planner's proposal looks like "finish what's started."
184	- **CHURNING**: any of the following:
185	  - helpers added in ≥2 of last K iters AND sorry count net unchanged or down by <1 per 2 iters AND no structural change in approach;
186	  - PARTIAL prover status ≥3 of last K iters;
187	  - **plan-phase-only meta-pattern**: ≥3 consecutive iters with **zero prover dispatches** on this route (no `Foo.lean` ever appearing in `## Current Objectives`). Pure planning rounds — re-blueprinting, re-strategizing, re-organizing — without ever firing a prover is the textbook stall. Each such iter individually shows "structural change in approach" (so the first clause fails), but the empirical signature is exactly what CHURNING was designed to flag. Use this clause when the planner is in a "we keep refactoring but never test it" pattern.
188	- **STUCK**: sorry count unchanged across K iters AND prover statuses include INCOMPLETE OR recurring blocker phrase across ≥3 iters. OR: helpers added without any sorry-elimination across K iters.
189	- **UNCLEAR**: route is fresh (< K iters of data) OR signals are ambiguous.
190	
191	If multiple rules match a route, pick the worse verdict (CHURNING > CONVERGING; STUCK > CHURNING).
192	
193	## Recommended actions per verdict
194	
195	For CHURNING or STUCK, your report names ONE primary corrective TYPE. The planner consults the catalog for the matching subagent.
196	
197	- **Blueprint expansion** — the chapter's proof sketch is likely under-specified; the planner should expand it (via the appropriate blueprint-writing subagent in their catalog) before more prover work.
198	- **Mathlib analogy consult** — the project may be using a parallel API or wrong predicate; the planner should consult Mathlib-idiom analysis on the route's load-bearing definitions.
199	- **Refactor** — the definition or file structure may be wrong; the planner should dispatch a structural subagent to restructure before more prover work.
200	- **Route pivot** — the strategic route may be wrong entirely; the planner should revise STRATEGY.md and pick a different route, then re-run any strategy critic in the catalog mid-iter to validate the pivot.
201	- **User escalation** — none of the above will work; the planner should pause and request user input. Use sparingly — only when no automated corrective will resolve the stall.
202	
203	Pick ONE primary corrective per CHURNING/STUCK route. Multiple are allowed when truly necessary, listed in priority order.
204	
205	## Report format
206	
207	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/progress-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
208	
209	```markdown
210	# Progress Critic Report
211	
212	## Slug
213	<slug>
214
Updated task #1 status
Updated task #2 status

# Strategy Critic Directive

## Slug
iter146

## Project goal

Formalize the nine protected declarations of Christian Merten's
Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
* `Genus.lean` — `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`
* `Jacobian.lean` — `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian`, `Jacobian.instGrpObj`,
  `Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus`, `Jacobian.instIsProper`,
  `Jacobian.instGeometricallyIrreducible`
* `AbelJacobi.lean` — `Jacobian.ofCurve`, `Jacobian.comp_ofCurve`,
  `Jacobian.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`

All nine signatures are frozen by the mathematician. The critical
non-trivial residual is `nonempty_jacobianWitness` (proof body), which
decomposes via `by_cases h : genus C = 0` into `genusZeroWitness`
(M2) + `positiveGenusWitness` (M3). The protected
`nonempty_jacobianWitness` quantifies over an arbitrary curve over
`Spec k` with no genus parameter and no $k$-rational-point hypothesis
(so over-`k̄` paths that assume `C(k) ≠ ∅` are mathematically false
on the protected signature).

## Strategy under review

DIRECTIVE_END

Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`). The non-trivial
residual is the body of `AlgebraicGeometry.nonempty_jacobianWitness`,
signature frozen at `(C : Over (Spec (.of k))) [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
[IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] : Nonempty (JacobianWitness C)`.
Strategy decomposes via `by_cases h : genus C = 0` into
`genusZeroWitness` (M2) + `positiveGenusWitness` (M3). The protected
declaration quantifies over an arbitrary curve with no
$k$-rational-point hypothesis, so over-`k̄` paths that assume
`C(k) ≠ ∅` are mathematically false on the protected signature.

## Blueprint summary (titles + one-line topics)

- `Cohomology_SheafCompose.tex` — sheaf-compose for `CommRing → Ring → Ab`.

- `AbelJacobi.tex` — three protected decls as projections from the Albanese witness.

chapter file; iter-145 routed the chart-algebra content into a

## Slug

## What you read

   (one-line index; the project's only reference is `challenge.lean`).
3. Blueprint chapter titles + one-line topics — read the first 5 lines
   of each `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` if needed (11 chapters; titles
   listed in the Blueprint summary section below for convenience).

## What you do NOT read

- `iter/iter-NNN/*.md` for any N (no iter sidecars).
- `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, any `task_results/*` other than
  your own report path.
- `proof-journal/`, `PROJECT_STATUS.md`, `PROGRESS.md`.

If you accidentally encounter any of these, ignore them.

## Project stated goal

Formalize the nine protected declarations of Christian Merten's
Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`). The non-trivial
residual is the body of `AlgebraicGeometry.nonempty_jacobianWitness`,
signature frozen at `(C : Over (Spec (.of k))) [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
[IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] : Nonempty (JacobianWitness C)`.
Strategy decomposes via `by_cases h : genus C = 0` into
`genusZeroWitness` (M2) + `positiveGenusWitness` (M3). The protected
declaration quantifies over an arbitrary curve with no
$k$-rational-point hypothesis, so over-`k̄` paths that assume
`C(k) ≠ ∅` are mathematically false on the protected signature.

## Blueprint summary (titles + one-line topics)

- `Cohomology_SheafCompose.tex` — sheaf-compose for `CommRing → Ring → Ab`.
- `Cohomology_StructureSheafAb.tex` — sheafification + Ext for Ab on opens.
- `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex` — sheaves of `k`-modules + Ext-with-`k`.
- `Cohomology_MayerVietoris.tex` — MV LES for sheaf cohomology + Čech-acyclicity.
- `Differentials.tex` — relative cotangent presheaf + `smooth_locally_free_omega`.
- `Genus.tex` — `genus C := finrank_k H^1(C, O_C)`.
- `Jacobian.tex` — Jacobian + M2/M3 decomposition + iter-145 Route A reconciliation.
- `Rigidity.tex` — `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` (scheme-level Mumford §4).
- `RigidityKbar.tex` — M2.a `rigidity_over_kbar` (k-agnostic) + NEW iter-145 subsection "Chart-algebra piece (ii) first-class decomposition" (L1773–L1944, 5 `\lean{...}` blocks tagging the iter-145 ChartAlgebra.lean placeholders).
- `AlgebraicJacobian_Cotangent_GrpObj.tex` — pointer chapter for `Cotangent/GrpObj.lean` (iter-145 EXCISE left 5 stale `\item`s pointing to deleted decls).
- `AbelJacobi.tex` — three protected decls as projections from the Albanese witness.

NOTE: there is NO `AlgebraicJacobian_Cotangent_ChartAlgebra.tex`
chapter file; iter-145 routed the chart-algebra content into a
subsection of `RigidityKbar.tex` rather than a new chapter.
`blueprint/src/content.tex` does NOT `\input` such a file. Iter-146
plan agent is aware; flag if this routing introduces strategic
incoherence.

2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
10	    review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
11	    present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
12	    multi-route coverage.
13	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
14	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
15	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
16	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
17	    what the directive lists.
18	  - **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` or ``\mathlibok``
19	    markers** (no "after writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no
20	    "verification: confirm `\leanok` is present"). The writer's
21	    descriptor forbids it — ``\leanok`` is managed by the
22	    deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase, ``\mathlibok`` by the review
23	    agent. A directive that asks the writer to add markers puts it in
24	    a rule conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the
25	    "verification" instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok
26	    runs. Leave marker concerns out of the writer's directive entirely.
27	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
28	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
29	    writer with TWO globs:
30	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
31	      --write-domain 'references/**'
32	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
33	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
34	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
35	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
36	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
37	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
38	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
39	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
40	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
41	  - After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
42	    in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to
43	    confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume
44	    the writer fixed everything.
45	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
46	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
47	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
48	    strategy-level issue.
49	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
50	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
51	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
52	---
53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
107	\end{theorem}
108	
109	\begin{proof}
110	  \uses{thm:another_result}
111	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
112	  so the dependency graph stays accurate.
113	\end{proof}
114	```
115	
116	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
117	
118	## Rules
119	
120	### What you CAN do
